original_hypothesis.title: Last-Hour Voting Restriction Bypass via deposit_for Triggering Poke for Any Voted Token
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: VoterV3.vote() restricts voting in the last hour of an epoch (epochVoteEnd check) for non-whitelisted, non-AVM tokens. However, VoterV3.poke() lacks this restriction — it only checks epochVoteStart. VotingEscrow.deposit_for() has no authentication requirement and triggers poke() for any voted token. Since msg.sender for poke() is the VE contract (passes `msg.sender == _ve` check), anyone can force a vote weight recalculation during the restricted last hour by depositing 1 wei into any voted token.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The epochVoteEnd restriction was designed to prevent last-minute vote sniping. The deposit_for→poke path completely bypasses this restriction, allowing vote weight manipulation after the voting window closes.

proof_of_signal.pattern: poke() only checks `_timestamp <= epochVoteStart` (line 139), NOT epochVoteEnd; contrast vote() which checks `_timestamp >= epochVoteEnd` (line 161)

preconditions: Target token has active votes in the current epoch
Current time is within the last-hour restriction window (epochVoteEnd to epochNext)
Attacker has at least 1 wei of BLACK token

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: deposit_for(tokenId, 1) → voted[tokenId] is true → IVoter(voter).poke(tokenId) → poke calls _vote which recalculates weights
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No admin control over poke timing restrictions
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Last NO_VOTING_WINDOW (300 seconds) of each epoch

operational_questions: Can deposit_for with minimal value trigger meaningful weight changes?
What is the practical impact of recalculated weights in the last hour?
Does the timing align with AVM voting window?
validation_requirements: Confirm deposit_for(tokenId, 1) triggers poke during restricted window
Test weight recalculation magnitude with varying deposit sizes

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker identifies high-value voted tokens during the epoch
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker accumulates BLACK tokens during the voting window
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: In the last hour, attacker calls deposit_for(targetTokenId, largeAmount) — tokens go into target's lock permanently
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Poke recalculates vote weights with inflated balance, shifting gauge emissions
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Capital required for meaningful weight change; tokens are locked permanently

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Last-minute gauge weight manipulation affects emission distribution
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No on-chain fix without upgrading VoterV3 or VotingEscrow

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: Minimal (1 wei) for trigger; substantial for meaningful impact
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Must be within epochVoteEnd to epochNext window (300 seconds with WEEK=1800)

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: deposit_for calls during last-hour window followed by weight changes
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Vote weight recalculations during restricted voting period
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Small deposits into others' tokens during last hour

validation_result.mechanism: Missing epochVoteEnd check in poke() bypassed via permissionless deposit_for()
validation_result.impact_summary: Anyone can bypass the last-hour voting restriction by calling deposit_for() on any voted token, triggering poke() from the VE contract which recalculates vote weights with inflated balances during the restricted window.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis correctly identifies a timing restriction bypass. VoterV3.vote() explicitly checks `_timestamp >= epochVoteEnd(_timestamp)` and reverts for non-whitelisted, non-AVM tokens during the last 300 seconds of each epoch. However, VoterV3.poke() only checks `_timestamp <= epochVoteStart(_timestamp)` (the start-of-epoch dead window) and completely omits the epochVoteEnd check. VotingEscrow.deposit_for() is permissionless (no isApprovedOrOwner check) and automatically calls IVoter(voter).poke(_tokenId) when voted[_tokenId] is true. When called from VE, msg.sender == _ve passes the auth check in poke(). This creates a clear bypass: during the restricted window, anyone can call deposit_for(targetTokenId, amount) to trigger poke(), which calls _vote() to recalculate weights using the new (inflated) balanceOfNFT. The epochVoteEnd restriction in vote() is rendered ineffective.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Three code paths converge to create this bypass: (1) deposit_for has no auth check and triggers poke for voted tokens, (2) poke accepts msg.sender == _ve, (3) poke lacks the epochVoteEnd timing restriction present in vote(). All three are confirmed in the source code.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No isApprovedOrOwner check — anyone can deposit for any tokenId. When voted[_tokenId] is true, it calls poke() with msg.sender = address(this) (the VE contract), which passes the _ve auth check in poke().

Only checks epochVoteStart (start-of-epoch dead window) but does NOT check epochVoteEnd (end-of-epoch restriction). Contrast with vote() which explicitly checks `_timestamp >= epochVoteEnd(_timestamp)`. This allows poke to execute during the last 300 seconds when vote() would revert.
Shows the epochVoteEnd restriction that is present in vote() but absent in poke(). This is the intended security control that gets bypassed.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker calls VotingEscrow.deposit_for(targetTokenId, amount) during last 300s of epoch
2. deposit_for requires _value > 0, _locked.amount > 0, lock not expired — no owner check
5. voted[_tokenId] is true (target was previously voted), so IVoter(voter).poke(_tokenId) is called
6. In VoterV3.poke(), msg.sender == address(VotingEscrow) == _ve, so auth passes
7. poke() only checks epochVoteStart (passes since we're past start), NOT epochVoteEnd
8. _vote() calls _reset() clearing old bribe deposits and weights
9. _vote() reads balanceOfNFT(_tokenId) which now reflects the inflated balance
10. _vote() recalculates pool weights proportionally with higher total, deposits to bribes
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The defect is in VoterV3.poke() which lacks the `epochVoteEnd` timing check. The entry point deposit_for() is intentionally permissionless (to allow anyone to add to any lock). The combination of permissionless deposit_for → automatic poke trigger → missing timing check in poke creates an exploitable path to bypass the last-hour voting restriction.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment assumptions needed — the vulnerability exists in the code logic regardless of configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack is realistic and low-cost: (1) Attacker identifies a voted tokenId for a pool they want to boost. (2) During the last 300 seconds of the epoch (when vote() would revert for non-whitelisted tokens), attacker calls deposit_for(targetTokenId, largeAmount). (3) The tokens are locked permanently in the target's lock — this is a real cost. (4) poke() recalculates weights with inflated balance. (5) The pool's share of emissions increases for that epoch. With WEEK=1800 (30 minutes), the restricted window is 300 seconds (5 minutes). The attacker must have BLACK tokens to deposit, and those tokens become locked in someone else's veNFT. However, if the attacker also controls the target veNFT, they can deposit into their own voted token to inflate their vote weight in the last minutes when competitors cannot respond.
validation_result.impact_assessment: The epochVoteEnd restriction was designed to prevent last-minute vote manipulation (vote sniping). This bypass renders it ineffective for any voted token. Impact includes: (1) Gauge emission manipulation — attacker can shift emissions to preferred pools in the last minutes. (2) Bribe extraction — by inflating vote weight for a pool with bribes, attacker captures disproportionate bribe rewards. (3) Unfair advantage — competitors cannot respond since vote() is restricted. The severity is amplified by the short epoch duration (WEEK=1800s), making the attack window a significant fraction of the epoch.
recommended_mitigations: Alternatively, skip the poke() call in deposit_for() during the restricted window: `if(voted[_tokenId] && block.timestamp < BlackTimeLibrary.epochVoteEnd(block.timestamp)) { IVoter(voter).poke(_tokenId); }`

Consider adding the same timing restriction to all VE functions that trigger poke() (increase_amount, increase_unlock_time, lockPermanent, merge) — though those require isApprovedOrOwner, they could still be self-exploited
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy all contracts with WEEK=1800. 2. Create two veNFTs: tokenA (attacker) and tokenB (victim). 3. tokenB votes for poolX during the voting window. 4. Advance time to the last 300 seconds of the epoch (past epochVoteEnd). 5. Verify that vote() reverts for tokenB with 'AVM_W'. 6. Call deposit_for(tokenB, largeAmount) from attacker address. 7. Observe that poke() succeeds and weights[poolX] increases by the amount corresponding to tokenB's new inflated balance. 8. Compare the weight change to demonstrate the bypass.
uncertainties: The exact economic incentive depends on the ratio of deposit cost to emission/bribe value gained — with permanent locking the cost is high
If the target veNFT is owned by the attacker, the cost is merely opportunity cost of locking more tokens, making the attack much more practical
additional_testing_needed: Quantify the minimum deposit needed to meaningfully shift gauge weights relative to totalWeight
Test whether the bribe checkpoint updates during poke correctly reflect the weight change for reward calculation
Verify that increase_amount (which also triggers poke but requires isApprovedOrOwner) has the same timing bypass for self-owned tokens
